nativeRange.habitat: Forest creeks, clear to tannin-stained streams and larger rivers; often over sandy or rocky substrate near the surface; also found in sluggish blackwater peat-swamp habitats

decorPreferences: robust background and midground planting (Java fern, Amazon sword, vallisneria)

diet.notes: Accepts most quality dried foods — flakes, micropellets, and slow-sinking granules form a good staple. Supplement regularly with frozen or live foods: bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, daphnia, brine shrimp, and mysis. Wild fish feed primarily on exogenous (surface-fallen) insects. Feed once or twice daily, offering only what is consumed within a few minutes.

goodWith: similarly sized peaceful cyprinids (giant danio, golden barb)
peaceful medium-sized cichlids (angelfish used with caution)
avoidWith: small nano fish or fry (predation risk at adult size)
Rasbora trilineata (common scissortail) mixed groups — species easily confused at point of sale

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species photo; confirm availability before use.

summary: The Greater Scissortail is a large, active schooling rasbora from Southeast Asian forest streams, reaching up to 17 cm. Distinguished from the similar Common Scissortail (R. trilineata) by its orange-yellow caudal fin lobes tipped with black and its considerably larger adult size. Easy to keep but demands a long tank and a tight-fitting lid.
careNotes: The Greater Scissortail is frequently confused with the Common Scissortail (Rasbora trilineata) in the trade; R. caudimaculata is identifiable by the orange-to-red flush at the base of each caudal fin lobe immediately before the black tip, and by its significantly larger adult size of up to 17 cm vs. ~8 cm for R. trilineata. A minimum tank length of 150 cm (5 ft) is strongly recommended — this is a powerful, fast-moving midwater swimmer that ranges widely and will not thrive when confined. Keep in groups of at least 6 to encourage natural schooling behaviour and reduce skittishness. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–12) best replicates forest-stream conditions; elevated nitrate accelerates decline, so weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are advised. The species is a confirmed jumper — a tight-fitting, gapless lid is essential. It is generally peaceful with fish too large to swallow, but small nano fish and fry are at risk from adults. Taxonomy note: the family placement has been updated to Danionidae (from the older Cyprinidae listed in legacy trade literature); order remains Cypriniformes.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer. No confirmed reports of successful spawning in home aquaria exist in the published literature. Breeding is presumed to require large, dimly lit soft-water setups with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Eggs are non-adhesive and likely require removal of adults immediately after spawning to prevent predation. Fry, if obtained, would be expected to accept infusoria and micro-foods initially. Captive breeding is considered very difficult to not achieved.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rasbora caudimaculata
primary.notes: Primary reference. Native range (Malay Peninsula, Greater Sunda Islands, Borneo), habitat (forest creeks, sandy substrate, blackwater tolerance), adult size, water parameters, diet, tank requirements, and breeding status sourced here. Page was temporarily unavailable at access time; data corroborated against FishBase and fishkeeper.co.uk.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora caudimaculata
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum size (17.0 cm SL), temperature range (20–26°C), native countries (Malaysia, Indonesia, lower Mekong basin), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), diet (exogenous insects, trophic level 3.3), and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Red Scissortail (Rasbora caudimaculata)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size to 18 cm, temperature 21–26°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness up to 12 dH, minimum group of 6, jumping risk, nitrate sensitivity, no captive breeding reports, and compatible food types.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Greater scissortail
additional.notes: Cross-checked caudal-fin coloration (orange-to-red flush preceding black fin-lobe tips), adult size (~15 cm TL), and habitat adaptability (fast-flowing forest streams to sluggish blackwater peat swamps). Taxonomy: Danionidae, order Cypriniformes.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Greater Scissortail Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (pH 6–7, dGH 2–12, temperature 20–26°C), minimum tank length (150 cm), diet, min group size (6–8), and egg-scattering breeding notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; no confirmed captive spawnings documented in home aquaria. Presumed to require large, dim, soft-water setups with fine plants. Adults must be removed post-spawning to protect eggs.
